PARAGRAPH 1
Here is the efficient Python function designed to filter historical dates based on a specific century requirement. The logic relies on extracting the year component from each date string and calculating which century that year belongs to. A century is defined as a period of one hundred years, starting from year one of the century number and ending at year one hundred of that same number. For example, the nineteenth century encompasses years from one thousand eight hundred one to one thousand nine hundred. The function iterates through the provided list of date strings, parses the four-digit year, and determines if it falls within the mathematical range defined by the input century argument. This approach ensures high performance even when processing large datasets containing thousands or millions of entries because the operation involves simple integer arithmetic and string slicing rather than complex object creation or heavy library calls. The code handles the edge cases where a year might be exactly on the boundary between two centuries, such as the year one thousand nine hundred which belongs to the nineteenth century, not the twentieth. By using list comprehension, we can create the resulting list in a single pass, which is a standard optimization technique in Python for filtering operations. The function accepts a list of strings formatted as year hyphen month hyphen day and an integer representing the target century. It returns a new list containing only the date strings that satisfy the century condition. If the input list is empty or if no dates match the criteria, the function will return an empty list without raising any errors. This robustness is essential for research applications where data quality might vary or where specific centuries might have no recorded events in the dataset. The implementation avoids importing external date libraries to maintain speed and simplicity, relying instead on the predictable structure of the ISO date format. Researchers can easily integrate this function into larger data processing pipelines or use it as a standalone utility for quick filtering tasks. The algorithm has a linear time complexity relative to the number of dates provided, meaning the processing time grows directly with the size of the input list. This linear scaling ensures that the function remains responsive even as the dataset expands significantly over time. No complex regular expressions are used, which further reduces the computational overhead for each individual date check. The solution is straightforward, readable, and adheres to standard Python coding conventions for clarity and maintainability. Users should ensure that the input date strings are valid and correctly formatted before passing them to the function to avoid potential parsing errors. The function does not modify the original list, preserving the integrity of the source data while providing a filtered view. This non-destructive behavior is
